title: Product Rules in Semidefinite Programming
speaker: Rajat Mittal
abstract: Semidefinite programming bounds are widely used in combinatorial optimization, quantum computing and complexity theory. The first semidefinite programming bound to gain fame is the so-called theta number developed by Lov\'asz to compute the Shannon capacity of the five-cycle graph. The semidefinite relaxation for the maximal cut problem has led to the near ubiquitous use of semidefinite programming in designing approximation algorithms.

proof_stages.label: Motivating multiplicative SDP phenomena
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates product rules by recalling SDPs such as Lovasz theta for independent sets and semidefinite values of nonlocal/XOR games, where known product or parallel-repetition theorems give multiplicativity.

proof_stages.label: Kronecker product of equality-form SDPs
proof_stages.summary: A standard equality-form SDP is specified by an objective matrix, constraint matrices, and right-hand sides, and the product SDP is defined by tensoring objectives, every pair of constraints, and right-hand sides, yielding an automatic lower bound from tensor-product feasible solutions.

proof_stages.label: From previous equality criteria to inequality-sensitive products
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews earlier sufficient product criteria and explains why applications with inequalities require a different product notion that multiplies equality constraints with equalities and inequality constraints with inequalities rather than all mixed pairs.

proof_stages.label: Main structural hypotheses for the generalized SDP
proof_stages.summary: After dualizing the generalized SDP, the announced sufficient hypotheses are that the objective is anti-block-diagonal, equality constraints are block-diagonal, inequality constraints are anti-block-diagonal, and the objective lies in the positive cone generated by the inequality matrices.

proof_stages.label: Duality strategy for proving exact multiplicativity
proof_stages.summary: Since primal tensor products give one inequality, strong duality reduces the reverse inequality to constructing a feasible dual solution for the product from feasible dual solutions of the two factors, allowing a new inequality multiplier vector because it does not affect the objective.

proof_stages.label: Tensor construction of the product dual solution
proof_stages.summary: Using positivity of the factor dual slack matrices and closure properties of the semidefinite cone under tensorial combinations, the proof expands the product slack and uses the positive conic representation of the objective to choose nonnegative product inequality multipliers.

proof_stages.label: Block/anti-block spectral sign-flip lemma
proof_stages.summary: The remaining positivity condition is obtained from the block-diagonal/anti-block-diagonal structure by observing that eigenvectors can be sign-flipped between the two relevant matrices without changing eigenvalues.

proof_stages.label: Sharpness, variants, applications, and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker shows that the positive-cone condition cannot simply be omitted, notes variants with inequality right-hand sides when primal tensor feasibility is checked, applies the framework to the Feige-Lovasz nonlocal-game SDP, and closes with open problems such as a positive-semidefinite-objective analogue and SDP composition rules.

examples.name: Maximum independent set and the Lovasz theta SDP
examples.note: The independent-set integer program and its Lovasz-theta SDP relaxation make semidefinite programming and multiplicativity concrete, including the later clarification via the graph product.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker formulates maximum independent set as an integer program, relaxes it to the Lovasz theta SDP, discusses the constraints and normalization, and relates the product definition to the product graph.

examples.name: Quantum XOR/nonlocal games
examples.note: Two-prover games, illustrated by a coloring-consistency check, are used to show another setting where an SDP exactly captures a value and satisfies a product rule under parallel repetition.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker sets up the two-prover nonlocal/XOR game model, writes the corresponding SDP form for binary XOR games, explains the matrix/diagonal constraints, and discusses its parallel-product value.

examples.name: Feige-Lovasz SDP for two-prover games
examples.note: The speaker uses the Feige-Lovasz nonlocal-game SDP to illustrate how block-diagonal equality constraints, anti-block-diagonal inequality constraints, and sign-expansion of absolute values fit the theorem's hypotheses.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker takes the Feige-Lovasz SDP for two-prover games, lays out its question-answer matrix structure, classifies its constraints as block or anti-block diagonal, removes modulus constraints via sign choices, and argues the product theorem applies.
